content.heroSubtitle: Is AI actually the right call here?
heroBody: This scorecard helps Acme Agency teams evaluate whether a workflow is a genuine candidate for AI or automation — before committing budget or time.
Score any workflow across eight suitability factors to get a clear, defensible recommendation:

heroBullets.label: Protect client relationships
heroBullets.text: by keeping high-stakes, judgment-intensive work human-led.

heroBullets.label: Capture real efficiency gains
heroBullets.text: only where volume, structure, and low error-stakes align.

heroBullets.label: Build a track record
heroBullets.text: of AI decisions that are auditable and easy to explain.

content.heroNote: Use this in client discovery sessions, internal sprint planning, or quarterly AI audits.
content.contactCta: Contact Acme Agency to discuss next steps for this workflow.

4.title: Is there a human quality gate before the output is used?
4.sub: AI outputs should always pass through a review step — especially anything client-facing.

6.title: What is the consequence if this output is wrong?
6.sub: Higher stakes require tighter human oversight. Never automate decisions your client can't recover from.

no-fit.title: Fix the process before adding AI
no-fit.body: The workflow lacks the structure, volume, or verifiability that AI requires. Adding AI to a broken process just makes it break faster. Redesign first.

ai-system.title: Strong case for a custom AI system
ai-system.body: High volume, structured inputs, and manageable risk make this a compelling candidate for a purpose-built AI workflow. Define your success metrics, then scope the build.

workflows.title: Client Intake & Onboarding
workflows.desc: Processing new client inquiries, contracts, and setup tasks.

workflows.title: Reporting & Insights
workflows.desc: Generating weekly/monthly reports for internal teams and clients.

workflows.title: Content Production
workflows.desc: Writing, editing, and publishing blog posts, social copy, and email campaigns.

workflows.title: Campaign Briefing
workflows.desc: Translating strategy into creative briefs for design and copy teams.

workflows.title: Design Asset Creation
workflows.desc: Producing on-brand visual assets for digital and print.

1.intro: Client intake and onboarding involves collecting information, setting up accounts, and communicating next steps. Consider how structured your intake form or CRM fields are.

hints.1: How many new clients do you onboard per week on average?
hints.2: Do clients fill out a standard intake form, or does it vary by account manager?
hints.3: Can you check whether an onboarding task is complete without expert judgment?
hints.4: Does a team lead review the setup before the client is notified?
hints.5: Is the bottleneck that there are too many intakes, or that each one requires custom thinking?
hints.6: What happens if a client account is set up incorrectly?
hints.7: Do you have past onboarding records or templates to reference?
hints.8: Does a completed onboarding always look roughly the same?

2.intro: Reporting workflows typically involve pulling data, summarizing performance, and formatting outputs. Consistency of data sources is the key variable here.

hints.1: How frequently are these reports produced?
hints.2: Does each report follow the same template, or does it change by client?
hints.3: Is it easy to tell if a report is accurate without domain expertise?
hints.4: Does someone sign off on the report before it goes to the client?
hints.5: Is the slow part pulling the data, or interpreting it?
hints.6: What happens if a report contains an error the client notices?
hints.7: Do you have historical data and past reports to reference?
hints.8: Do all reports follow a consistent structure?

3.intro: Content production covers the creation of written assets. The key question is how much of the work is templated versus requiring genuine creative judgment.

hints.1: How many pieces of content are produced per week?
hints.2: Does each piece start from a brief or template, or from scratch?
hints.3: Can you objectively tell when a piece of content is 'done'?
hints.4: Does an editor or strategist review before publishing?
hints.5: Is the bottleneck volume of content, or quality of each piece?
hints.6: What happens if a published piece contains a factual error or tone issue?
hints.7: Do you have a brand voice guide, past content, and templates?
hints.8: Does finished content typically follow a predictable format?

4.intro: Campaign briefing turns strategy into actionable direction for creative teams. The challenge is often translating qualitative client context into structured instructions.

hints.1: How often are new campaign briefs created?
hints.2: Do briefs follow a standard template?
hints.3: Is there a clear quality standard a brief must meet?
hints.4: Does a strategist or account lead review the brief before it goes to creative?
hints.5: Is the bottleneck writing the brief, or deciding what the campaign should be?
hints.6: What's the consequence of a brief that misses the client's intent?
hints.7: Do you have past briefs and brand guidelines to draw from?
hints.8: Do completed briefs follow a consistent structure?

5.intro: Design asset production involves creating visual content at scale. AI can assist with templated formats but struggles with original creative direction.

hints.1: How many individual design assets are produced per week?
hints.2: Does each asset start from a template or an established design system?
hints.3: Is there a clear brand standard to check the output against?
hints.4: Does a designer or creative director approve before delivery?
hints.5: Is the slowness due to volume of requests or complexity of each one?
hints.6: What happens if an off-brand asset is delivered to a client?
hints.7: Do you have a robust design system, templates, and past assets?
hints.8: Do all delivered assets follow a predictable format or layout?
